prompt: Can you find datasets related to Business/industrial management and organisation in Germany during the 1960s?
answer:
Below is a non‐exhaustive roundup of publicly available (or publicly documented) data sources that you can use to study business and industrial management/organization in West Germany in the 1960s.  In most cases you will have to go via a research data centre or order historical publications, but all of the series below do cover the 1960–1970 period.

1. Destatis (Statistisches Bundesamt) – Historical Time Series  
   • “Historische Zeitreihen, Unternehmen und Arbeitsstätten” in GENESIS‐Online covers manufacturing, trade and service enterprises by sector, number of establishments, employees, sales, fixed assets, investment, etc., back to 1960.  
   • Publication series “Statistisches Jahrbuch für die Bundesrepublik Deutschland” (annual) and “Historische Tabellen zur Wirtschafts- und Sozialstatistik” (multi-volume) contain tabulated data on employment, corporate size classes, investment, capacity utilization, productivity.  

   How to get it:  
   – GENESIS-Online portal (register for free access to many time series; the historical tables require a small fee or university subscription)  
   – On-site or postal order from Destatis library for the printed Jahrbücher and historical tables  

2. “ifo Business Survey” (ifo Konjunkturumfrage)  
   • The Munich ifo Institute has been polling a panel of manufacturing firms since 1949 on order books, production expectations, inventory, export prospects, etc.  Although most users know only the aggregate “Business Climate Index,” the underlying microdata (firms × monthly/quarterly responses) exist from the 1950s onward.  

   How to get it:  
   – Contact the ifo Data Archive (ifo Institut für Wirtschaftsforschung, Forschungsdatenzentrum)  
   – There is a formal application procedure for historical microdata  

3. Deutsche Bundesbank – Corporate Sector Statistics  
   • The Bundesbank published annual and quarterly “Konjunkturstatistiken” including capacity utilization, output, investment, and inventories in manufacturing, mining and energy.  Some series go back to the mid‐1950s.  

   How to get it:  
   – Bundesbank Statistics Division or Deutsche Bundesbank Research Data Centre  

4. Mikrozensus (annual household–person microdata)  
   • Germany’s annual microcensus has run continuously since 1950.  From it you can extract occupational categories, managerial versus non-managerial staff shares, firm size (employer vs. non-employer), industry, region, etc.  

   How to get it:  
   – Federal Statistical Office’s Research Data Centre (FDZ) for anonymised person-level data  
   – Many German university data archives hold the microcensus on site  

5. Hoppenstedt Firmendaten (Historical Corporate Directories)  
   • The Hoppenstedt “Konzern- und Firmen-Handbuch” series documented all medium-sized and large German firms.  From the 1960s editions you can scrape or manually extract: headquarters, subsidiaries, employees, turnover, board members.  

   How to get it:  
   – Many university libraries hold bound volumes for each year  
   – Some business history research departments have digitised versions  

6. OECD and UNIDO Historical Statistics  
   • OECD “Main Economic Indicators” and “OECD Industrial Outlook” include aggregate‐level time series for Germany from the early 1960s (production indices, capacity utilisation, investment, productivity).  
   • UNIDO Yearbook of Industrial Statistics contains data by industrial branch back into the 1960s.  

   How to get it:  
   – OECD iLibrary  
   – UNIDO website or libraries with UN documents  

7. Archive of the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs (Bundesarchiv B 322)  
   • If you are looking for qualitative and some quantitative material on industrial policy, management reforms, organizational debates you can consult the ministry files from the 1960s.  These sometimes contain commissioned surveys, expert reports, and tabulations of management structures in key sectors.  

   How to get it:  
   – Bundesarchiv in Koblenz or Berlin; request files under the file-reference “B 322”  

8. Industry Association Yearbooks (VDMA, VDI, DIHK)  
   • Branchenverbände such as the Verband Deutscher Maschinen- und Anlagenbau (VDMA), der Industrieverband, or the Deutsche Industrie- und Handelskammertag (DIHK) produced annual statistics on production, foreign trade, export markets, employment, and sometimes on management practices (e.g. apprenticeship ratios, training budgets).  

   How to get it:  
   – Association headquarters or in many cases their historical statistics are deposited at the German National Library or state libraries  

—  
Practical steps to proceed:  
1. Decide whether you need aggregate time series (Destatis, Bundesbank, OECD, UNIDO) or firm/plant-level microdata (ifo survey, Mikro­zensus, Hoppenstedt).  
2. Register with the relevant research data centre (Destatis FDZ, ifo FDZ, Bundesbank, DIW, university library).  
3. Order printed historical Jahrbücher or subscribe to GENESIS-Online for time series.  
4. If you need qualitative material on management/organizational reform debates, plan an on-site visit to the Bundesarchiv (B 322) or relevant ministerial archive.  

All of the above sources will give you numerical/statistical foundation for business and industrial management in West Germany during the 1960s. If you are at a German university you will typically have free or low-cost access via your institution to most of the FDZ centres and archived Jahrbücher. Good luck with your research!

## 2. German Data Forum (RatSWD) and GESIS Data Archive

prompt: Can you find datasets related to Media in Germany during the 1960s?
answer:
Here are a number of publicly‐available data sources and archives you can draw on if you’re researching media in Germany in the 1960s.  I’ve grouped them into four categories—official statistics, survey/microdata, press‐and‐broadcast corpora, and bibliographic/catalogue data—and given pointers on how to access each.

1. Official Statistics (West Germany & East Germany)  
   • Statistisches Bundesamt (Destatis) – “Historische Zeitreihen”:  
     – Contains annual figures for Federal Republic households on radio sets, television sets, hours of broadcasting, number of transmitters, etc., from the 1950s through the 1970s.  
     – Access via the Destatis website: https://www.destatis.de → “Themen” → “Zeitreihen” → search “Rundfunk, Fernsehen”  
     – Some series are downloadable as Excel or CSV; older series are in PDF in the printed Historical Yearbooks.  
   • Statistisches Jahrbuch der DDR (Ostdeutsche Jahrbücher)  
     – Annual statistical yearbooks of the GDR include circulation figures for state newspapers, number of radio/TV sets in East‐German households, broadcast hours by state station (DFF), etc.  
     – Digital scans of volumes 1955–1990 are often available in regional library digital collections (e.g. SLUB Dresden) or through the Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek.  

2. Survey-Microdata on Audience & Usage  
   • IfD Allensbach (Allensbacher Markt- und Werbeträgeranalyse / Radiound Fernsehumfragen)  
     – Continuous survey since the 1950s of radio and TV usage, audience reach, media‐preference questions, broken down by age, occupation, region.  
     – Microdata for select years in the 1960s can be requested via the GESIS Data Archive (GESIS-ID: 4155 for the Radiound Fernsehumfrage).  
     – GESIS catalog: https://search.gesis.org/  
   • Zensus 1961 & 1970 (West Germany)  
     – The federal census questionnaires included a question on “household ownership of radio receiver” and (from 1961 onward) “television set.”  
     – Microdata are available—codebooks and anonymized records—via GESIS.  
   • Mannheim Media Panel (historical supplements)  
     – Although the main MMAP panel begins later, the university’s archive holds small surveys on press usage in 1962–1968; inquire with the University of Mannheim’s media research department.  

3. Broadcast & Press Corpora  
   • ZEFYS – Zeitungsinformationssystem (Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin)  
     – Digitized issues of major Berlin and national newspapers (e.g. Berliner Zeitung, BZ, Frankfurter Allgemeine ca.1960–69).  
     – While not “structured data,” you can download OCR-text at article level for your own coding.  
     – https://zefys.staatsbibliothek-berlin.de/  
   • Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek / Deutsche Nationalbibliothek  
     – Scanned copies of magazines (Stern, Der Spiegel, Quick) from the 1960s, with metadata (issue, date, pagination) in machine-readable format (METS/ALTO).  
   • ARD Program Logs (Bundesarchiv)  
     – The Bundesarchiv holds program logs for West‐German public broadcasters (e.g. WDR, NDR) including daily schedules, program descriptions, broadcast times.  
     – You can request digitized indexes; no direct online download, but finding aids are available on the Bundesarchiv portal.  

4. Bibliographic & Periodical Catalogs  
   • Zeitschriften-Datenbank (ZDB)  
     – Union catalog of all German‐language serials, including those active in the 1960s. Metadata (start/stop year, publication place, publisher, frequency) is available via API.  
     – https://www.zdb-services.de/  
   • K10plus / GBV-Verbundkatalog  
     – For full holdings information on German-language magazines and newspapers—you can harvest MARCXML records to build a title‐level dataset (issuing frequency, library counts, holdings dates).  

How to proceed  
1. Go to the GESIS Data Catalog (https://search.gesis.org/) and search keywords “media,” “Rundfunk,” “Fernsehen” + “1960.” You will find the Allensbach and census microdata plus related surveys.  
2. Download the Destatis “Historische Zeitreihen” tables on radio/TV sets and broadcast hours directly from the Destatis website (often under “Publikationen → Fachreihen → Zeitreihen”).  
3. For press and broadcast content, use ZEFYS and the Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek to scrape or export OCR texts and metadata; for ARD program logs you will need to request the inventories from the Bundesarchiv.  
4. Enrich your project with bibliographic metadata from ZDB and GBV via their APIs.

Taken together, these sources will give you quantitative time series on device ownership and broadcast output, microdata on audience behavior, and full-text/metadata corpora of what was actually published or aired in Germany throughout the 1960s.

     – 41111‐0002: Studienanfänger je Semester

   • How to access:  
     – Many university libraries hold scanned PDFs of the 1960s yearbooks.

4) UNESCO Institute for Statistics (UIS) – Historical Education Data

     – Gross Tertiary Enrollment Ratio (% of 18–22-year-olds enrolled in tertiary)  
     – Number of tertiary graduates by broad field (science & engineering, humanities, etc.)  
   • How to access:  
     – Go to http://data.uis.unesco.org/  
     – Filter by country (Germany) and years 1960–1969

5) GESIS Data Archive – German Microcensus (1962, 1971)  
   • What it contains: Individual-level microdata from the national Microcensus household survey, including

     – Enrollment status (still in education vs. labour force)

6) Federal Institute for Vocational Education and Training (BIBB) – Historical Continuing Education Statistics  
   • What it contains: Data on further and continuing education (Weiterbildung) providers and participants, especially Volkshochschulen and company-based courses  
   • How to access:

       https://www.bibb.de  

7) Deutscher Volkshochschul-Verband (DVV) – Annual Reports

   • How to access:

     – Key search term: “DVV Jahresbericht 196* Volkshochschule”  

8) Bundesministerium für Bildung und Forschung (BMBF) – Historical Educational Reports  
   • What it contains: Policy reports and statistics on higher education expansion in the 1960s (e.g. the 1965 Hochschulstrukturkommission)  
   • How to access:

9) OECD Historical Publications

—  
If you are doing quantitative analysis, I’d recommend starting with the Destatis GENESIS-Online tables for the core enrolment and graduate numbers, and then complementing them with the Microcensus data (GESIS) for individual-level breakdowns by social background and attendance. - Statistisches Jahrbuch (Statistical Yearbook of the Federal Republic of Germany)

  - Access: digitised copies are available in library collections and often on Destatis or national library portals.
  - Use when: scanning a broad set of historical tables quickly.

Federal and sectoral agencies

  - What: consolidated higher education statistics and longer time-series collated by the ministry. Useful summaries, sometimes with historical series.
  - Use when: you want ministry presentations/collated series or modern exports of historical series.
  - Link: https://www.bmbf.de/ (search for “Hochschulstatistik Zeitreihen”)

- Hochschulrektorenkonferenz (HRK) / German Rectors’ Conference
  - What: historical statistics about universities and colleges, numbers of students, foreign students etc.

- BIBB (Federal Institute for Vocational Education and Training)

International and comparative sources

  - What: international time series on enrolments by education level, where available for Germany in the 1960s.
  - Use when: you need comparable international indicators or a quick cross-check.

- OECD Education Statistics (historical)

Microdata (individual-level)
- Mikrozensus (Microcensus) and Census microdata
  - What: Microcensus is an annual household survey with education variables (occupation, highest qualification, school attendance). Microdata for the 1960s exist but are restricted.
  - Access: apply via the Research Data Centres (Forschungsdatenzentren) at Destatis or GESIS; usually require a project proposal and secure access conditions.

Regional / East Germany (GDR) data

Practical suggestions / next steps

prompt: Can you find datasets related to Higher and further education in Germany during the 1960s?
answer:
Here are concrete places and dataset leads you can follow for “higher and further education in Germany in the 1960s.” Because this is quite specific (sector + country + decade), you’ll usually find the 1960s data embedded in larger historical/statistical series rather than as a standalone “1960s dataset.”

---

## 1. German Federal Statistical Office (Destatis)

**What they have:** Official statistics on students, institutions, exams, staff, etc., for universities and (often) other tertiary institutions. Historical time series typically go back to the 1950s/60s.

**Key entry points:**

1. **Statistik der Studenten / Studierende**  
   Look for historical tables on:
   - Number of students (Studierende) by:
     - year (Jahr),
     - type of higher education institution (Universität, Technische Hochschule, Pädagogische Hochschule, Fachhochschule—though FHs become important slightly later),
     - subject group (Fächergruppe),
     - gender.
   - The 1960s are usually included in PDF or XLS tables that span 1950–2000 or similar.

2. **Bildungsstatistik – Historical Yearbooks**  
   Destatis publishes (or has digitized) older yearbooks:
   - *Fachserie 11: Bildung und Kultur* (later volumes; older material is in Jahrbücher).  
   - *Statistisches Jahrbuch für die Bundesrepublik Deutschland* (and pre‑1961 *Statistisches Jahrbuch für die Bundesrepublik*).  
   These contain tables on:
   - students and first‑year students in higher education,
   - graduates by field,
   - teaching staff,
   often with series starting in the 1950s.

3. **How to search:**  
   On the Destatis website (English or German):
   - Go to “Statistik nach Themen” → “Bildung, Forschung, Kultur” / “Education, Research, Culture”.
   - Then “Bildung und Kultur” → “Bildung und Bildungsstand” / “Hochschulen”.
   - Use search terms like:
     - `Hochschulen Zeitreihe 1960`
     - `Studierende 1950 1970`
     - `Fachserie 11 Hochschulen historische Reihen`
   Many tables are downloadable as XLS/CSV; older ones are often PDFs but still structured.

**Caveat:** Destatis covers the Federal Republic (West Germany). For East Germany, see section 4.

---

## 2. German Microdata & Research Data Centers

If you need more than aggregated tables:

### a) FDZ / RDC of the Statistical Offices (Forschungsdatenzentren)

- The **FDZ der Statistischen Ämter des Bundes und der Länder** provides access to detailed microdata and historical series, including education-related statistics.
- For historical university data, they might have:
  - Institutional-level counts,
  - region-level breakdowns,
  - possibly microdata on graduates or students.

**Steps:**
- Visit the FDZ website and search for:
  - `Hochschulstatistik`, `Bildungsstatistik`, `historisch`.
- Ask them (they accept research requests) about:
  - availability for the 1960s,
  - West vs East Germany coverage,
  - level of detail (micro vs macro).

Often you access the data via on‑site use or remote execution, not simple file download, but they may have ready-made “Scientific Use Files.”

### b) GESIS – Leibniz Institute for the Social Sciences

- GESIS is the main archive for German social science datasets.
- While they mostly host surveys, they also have “contextual/historical” data and occasionally digitized statistical series.

**Search terms on GESIS Data Search:**
- `Hochschulbildung 1960*`  
- `Bildungshistorische Daten Bundesrepublik 1950 1970`  
- `higher education Germany time series`  

Examples (titles change, but look for things like):
- “German Educational Expansion 1950–1990: Time Series on Enrollment and Attainment”
- Regional indicators including “students per 1000 inhabitants”, etc.

---

## 3. International Databases with Historical Education Series

These often have fewer variables but cover Germany back into the 1960s.

### a) OECD Historical Educational Data

The OECD has several datasets that include:
- **Enrolment in tertiary education** by country and year,
- sometimes by gender and type of program.

Look for:
- *OECD Education Database* (some series start in the 1960s),
- older publications like “Education at a Glance – Historical Time Series” or historical compendia.

Access:
- Via OECD.Stat (requires registration; free for many institutions),
- Search for:
  - `Enrolment by level of education – tertiary – 1960`
  - filter Country = Germany (often separated into “Germany (Fed. Rep.)” and “Germany (Dem. Rep.)” pre‑1990).

### b) UNESCO Institute for Statistics (UIS)

UIS provides:
- Tertiary gross enrolment ratios,
- numbers of students and teachers by level of education,
with historical series that for some countries go back to the 1960s.

Search on UIS Data Explorer:
- Country: “Germany West” / “Germany (before unification)” and “German Democratic Republic” when available.
- Indicators:
  - `Tertiary education, all programmes, Enrolment (number)`
  - `Tertiary education, Gross enrolment ratio`

Export as CSV.

---

## 4. East Germany (GDR / DDR) Higher Education Data

To cover the **GDR** (German Democratic Republic), you’ll often need different sources:

### a) Former GDR Statistical Publications

Look for digitized volumes such as:
- *Statistisches Jahrbuch der Deutschen Demokratischen Republik*, 1950s–1980s.
These contain tables on:
- university students,
- graduates,
- technical schools (Technische Schulen),
- engineering schools, etc.

Digitization:
- **German National Library (DNB)** and some university libraries have scanned yearbooks.
- The tables can be extracted manually into datasets (often they are consistent across years).

Search terms:
- `“Statistisches Jahrbuch der DDR” PDF`
- `Hochschulwesen DDR 1960 Statistik`

### b) DIW / Historical Data Compilations

The **German Institute for Economic Research (DIW Berlin)** and some scholars have compiled historical macroeconomic and social databases that include education indicators for both FRG and GDR.

Look for:
- “Historical macro-database Germany” or similar,
- DIW data documentation where variables may include:
  - tertiary students,
  - students per age group,
  - educational attainment of the population.

---

## 5. “Further Education” (Weiterbildung, Adult Education)

“Further education” is more difficult, as systematic national statistics on continuing/adult education were less standardized in the 1960s.

Possible directions:

1. **Volks­hochschulen (adult education centers)**  
   - The **Deutscher Volkshochschul-Verband (DVV)** and some regional associations have historical statistics on:
     - number of courses,
     - participants,
     - subject areas.
   - Coverage often goes back to the 1950s.

   Search:
   - `“Volkshochschulstatistik” 1960`
   - `DVV Statistik 1950 1970 PDF`

2. **Adult Education Surveys / Historical Compilations**
   - GESIS may host early survey data on adult education participation in West Germany from the late 1960s onward.
   - Look for:
     - `Weiterbildung Bundesrepublik 1960er`
     - `adult education Germany 1969` in GESIS Data.

3. **International Comparisons**
   - OECD and UNESCO sometimes reported participation in adult education programs, though for the 1960s coverage is patchy.
   - Check their historical yearbooks for aggregate indicators.

---

## 6. Examples of Specific Dataset Types You’re Likely to Find

You probably won’t find a single “off-the-shelf” CSV called “Germany higher & further education 1960s”; instead you’ll assemble from:

1. **Time series tables** (Destatis, DDR yearbooks):
   - Year × number of students at universities / teacher colleges / technical colleges.
   - Year × graduates by discipline.
   - Year × teaching staff numbers.

2. **International comparative series** (OECD, UIS):
   - Country-year data with:
     - tertiary enrolment,
     - enrolment ratios,
     - sometimes expenditure on higher education.

3. **Contextual or regional data** (FDZ, DIW, some GESIS files):
   - State (Bundesland) × year × tertiary enrolment,
   - Students per 10,000 inhabitants by state,
   - Distinguishing West/East Germany.

4. **Adult/further education:**
   - Volkshochschule courses/participants per year,
   - Occasional survey-based datasets on individual participation.

---

## 7. If you tell me more, I can narrow this down

It would help to know:
- Do you need: West Germany only, East Germany, or both?
- Level of detail: just national-level totals, or by state, institution type, field of study, gender?
- Format: are carefully-structured PDFs acceptable, or do you need ready-to-use CSV/Excel files?
- Is “further education” specifically adult/continuing education, or are Fachschulen/Fachhochschulen included?

If you answer these, I can suggest more precise datasets and, where possible, approximate the exact table names or even draft a step-by-step click path for Destatis, UIS, OECD, or GESIS.

prompt: Can you find datasets related to Political behaviour and attitudes in Germany during the 1960s?
answer:
Okay, finding datasets specifically *from* the 1960s on German political behavior and attitudes can be challenging.  Systematic survey research wasn't as widespread then as it is now. However, here's a breakdown of resources and datasets (or leads to datasets) that cover that period, categorized for clarity.  I'll also note the challenges and where to look for potentially related materials.

**I. Major Established Survey Series (with some 1960s coverage)**

These are the most likely places to find quantitative data, even if it's not *extensive* for the early 60s.

*   **Allensbach Ad Hoc Surveys (Allensbacher Ad-hoc-Erhebungen):** This is a *key* source. The Allensbach Institute (Institut für Demoskopie Allensbach) has been conducting surveys since 1947. While full access to raw data can be difficult, they publish detailed reports and summaries.  You'll need to investigate their archive for the 1960s specifically.
    *   **Website:** [https://www.ifd-allensbach.de/](https://www.ifd-allensbach.de/) (German language primarily, but they often have English summaries)
    *   **Availability:** Reports are available in academic libraries. Raw data access may require specific requests and fees.  Look for reports covering topics like political orientations, party preferences, attitudes towards the Cold War, economic beliefs, and social values.
*   **Emnid Surveys:** Emnid (now part of Kantar) is another long-running German polling institute.  Like Allensbach, they have historical data, but access to raw data is usually limited to published reports.
    *   **Website:** [https://www.kantar.com/de-DE/opinion/](https://www.kantar.com/de-DE/opinion/) (Look for their historical data sections)
    *   **Availability:**  Similar to Allensbach - library access to reports.
*   **Infratest dimap (now infratest burgo):** This institute also has a long history of polling.  Focus on their archive for the 1960s.
    *   **Website:** [https://www.infratest-dimap.de/](https://www.infratest-dimap.de/) (German language)
    *   **Availability:** Reports and summaries. Data access may be restricted.
*   **GESIS – Leibniz Institute for the Social Sciences:** GESIS is a major social science data archive in Germany. They archive data from many surveys, including some from the 1960s.  Their search portal is essential.
    *   **Website:** [https://www.gesis.org/en](https://www.gesis.org/en)
    *   **GESIS Data Explorer:** [https://www.gesis.org/data-explorer](https://www.gesis.org/data-explorer)  (This is where you'll actively search. Use keywords like "Germany," "political attitudes," "1960s," "Wahlen" (elections), "politische Einstellungen", etc.
    *   **Important GESIS Surveys to Check:**
        *   **German General Social Survey (ALLBUS):** While the ALLBUS started later, there may be earlier predecessor surveys or modules that touch on relevant topics from the 1960s.
        *   **Wahlstudien (Election Studies):** GESIS archives data from post-election surveys. Even if not directly attitudinal, they provide insights into voting behavior.

**II. Qualitative Data & Historical Sources**

These won't be neatly formatted datasets, but they are crucial for understanding the context and can provide rich insights.

*   **Political Archives:**
    *   **Bundesarchiv (German Federal Archives):**  [https://www.bundesarchiv.de/](https://www.bundesarchiv.de/)  (German language) This is a treasure trove of documents. Look for records from political parties, government ministries, and organizations active in the 1960s. You'll find speeches, internal memos, correspondence, and reports that reveal political thinking and public sentiment.  It requires archival research (often in person or via a researcher).
    *   **Landesarchive (State Archives):**  Each German state has its own archive.  These can contain regional-level data and perspectives.
    *   **Archive of Political Foundations (e.g., Friedrich Ebert Stiftung, Konrad Adenauer Stiftung):** These foundations often have extensive archives documenting political and social developments.
*   **Newspaper and Magazine Archives:**
    *   **Zeitungsarchive (Newspaper Archives):** Major German newspapers (e.g., *Süddeutsche Zeitung*, *Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung*, *Die Zeit*) have online archives.  Analyzing articles from the 1960s provides insights into public discourse and political issues. (Often requires subscription).
    *   **Der Spiegel:** The news magazine *Der Spiegel* was very influential.  Its archives are a valuable source.
*   **Oral History Projects:** Look for oral history interviews with people who lived through the 1960s in Germany.  These can provide personal accounts of political attitudes and experiences.
*   **Published Historical Studies:** Search academic databases (see section IV) for books and articles about German political history and society in the 1960s.  Researchers may have used primary sources that they can cite or that you can track down.

**III. Specific Topics & Potential Data Sources**

To help focus your search, consider these key themes from the 1960s in Germany:

*   **The Student Movement (Studentenbewegung):**  This was a major force. Look for materials related to the *Außerparlamentarische Opposition* (APO) – the extra-parliamentary opposition.  University archives may hold records of student organizations and protests.
*   **The Cold War and Ostpolitik:**  Attitudes towards East Germany and the Soviet Union were central.  Look for data related to public opinion on these issues.
*   **Economic Miracle (Wirtschaftswunder) & Social Change:**  The rapid economic growth and changing social norms of the 1960s had a significant impact on political attitudes.
*   **Post-War Guilt and Coming to Terms with the Past (Vergangenheitsbewältigung):**  Germany was grappling with its Nazi past.  Attitudes towards this were evolving.
*   **Formation of the Grand Coalition (Große Koalition):** The CDU/CSU and SPD formed a grand coalition in 1966-1969. This period is significant for understanding political dynamics.

**IV.  Academic Databases**

*   **JSTOR:** [https://www.jstor.org/](https://www.jstor.org/)
*   **ProQuest:** [https://www.proquest.com/](https://www.proquest.com/)
*   **Google Scholar:** [https://scholar.google.com/](https://scholar.google.com/)
*   **German Academic Databases:**
    *   **zbMATH Open:** [https://zbmath.org/](https://zbmath.org/) (Mathematics, but also related social sciences)
    *   **BASE (Bielefeld Academic Search Engine):** [https://www.base-search.net/](https://www.base-search.net/)
    *   **German National Library (Deutsche Nationalbibliothek):** [https://www.dnb.de/](https://www.dnb.de/)

*   **Official Statistics:** The *Statistisches Bundesamt* (Federal Statistical Office) has historical data, but it may not be broken down in precisely the way you need for political analysis.
    *   **Statistisches Bundesamt:** [https://www.destatis.de/EN/Themes/Countries-Regions/Germany/historical-statistics.html](https://www.destatis.de/EN/Themes/Countries-Regions/Germany/historical-statistics.html) – Search for publications from the 1960s.

**II. Archival Collections (Best Bet, Requires Research)**

These are the main places to look, but most require physical visits or commissioned research.

*   **Bundesarchiv (Federal Archives):** This is *the* central repository for German federal government records.  It holds a vast amount of material from the 1960s, including records of ministries, political parties, and organizations.
    *   **Bundesarchiv:** [https://www.bundesarchiv.de/EN/Home/home.html](https://www.bundesarchiv.de/EN/Home/home.html) – Their online finding aids are improving, but still incomplete.  You'll need to search their *Inventare* (finding aids) to identify relevant series (Bestände).  Look for records related to the Adenauer, Erhard, and Kiesinger governments.  Keywords: "Parteien" (parties), "Organisationen" (organizations), "Regierung" (government), specific ministries, etc.
*   **Landesarchive (State Archives):**  Each state has its own archive.  Important for regional political dynamics.
*   **Archive of Political Foundations:** (e.g., Friedrich Ebert Stiftung, Konrad Adenauer Stiftung, Heinrich Böll Foundation) These foundations often have archives documenting their own activities and related political developments.
    *   **Friedrich Ebert Stiftung Archive:** [https://www.fes.de/en/the-friedrich-ebert-stiftung/archive](https://www.fes.de/en/the-friedrich-ebert-stiftung/archive)
    *   **Konrad Adenauer Stiftung Archive:** [https://www.kas.de/en/web/about-us/archives](https://www.kas.de/en/web/about-us/archives)
*    **NS-Dokumentationszentrum München (Documentation Center for the History of National Socialism):** While focused on Nazism, this archive also has records related to the post-war political landscape and the reckoning with the past, which was a major theme in the 1960s.
    *   [https://www.nsdok.de/en/](https://www.nsdok.de/en/)

**III.  Datasets and Digital Resources (More Limited, but Useful)**

*   **Political Party Membership Data:**  Very hard to find comprehensive data.  Some research projects may have compiled partial datasets, but they are often not publicly available.  Look for academic publications that might reference such data.
*   **Election Results:**  Detailed election results are available, but often not in a readily analyzable format.
    *   **Bundeswahlleiter (Federal Returning Officer):** [https://www.bundeswahlleiter.de/en/](https://www.bundeswahlleiter.de/en/) - Has historical election results, but you may need to request data in a specific format.
*   **Newspaper Archives:** Digitized newspaper archives can provide valuable qualitative data.
    *   **German Newspaper Archive (ZDB):** [https://www.zdb-zbv.de/](https://www.zdb-zbv.de/) (German) -  Allows searching across many German newspapers.  Access may require a subscription.
*   **Social Science Research Projects:** Search for academic research projects conducted on German politics in the 1960s.  Researchers may have created datasets that they are willing to share (though this is not common).
    *   **GESIS – Leibniz Institute for the Social Sciences:** [https://www.gesis.org/en/](https://www.gesis.org/en/) – GESIS is a major social science research institute in Germany. Search their archive for relevant studies.
*   **German Historical Institute (GHI) Washington D.C.:** [https://www.ghi-dc.org/](https://www.ghi-dc.org/) – Often has research projects and resources related to German history.
*    **Zeitungsportal:** [https://www.zeitungsportal.de/](https://www.zeitungsportal.de/) –  A portal to digitized German newspapers.  Can be useful for finding articles related to political events.

**IV. Types of Data You Might Find (or Reconstruct)**

Even if you don't find ready-made datasets, you can *create* data from archival sources.  Consider these:

*   **Political Party Platforms & Statements:**  Textual data that can be analyzed using natural language processing.
*   **Voting Records (Bundestag/Landtag):**  If you can access parliamentary protocols, you can manually record how MPs voted on key issues.
*   **Cabinet Composition & Changes:**  Who held which portfolios and when.
*   **Membership of Important Commissions & Committees:**  Reveals power dynamics.
*   **Public Opinion Polls:** (Rare for the early 1960s, but they become more common later in the decade).
*   **Data on Political Protests & Demonstrations:**  Newspaper reports and police records can be used to reconstruct data on protests.
*   **Economic Indicators:** (GDP, unemployment, inflation) – Context for political decision-making. (From Statistisches Bundesamt).




**Challenges & Considerations:**

*   **Language:**  Most resources are in German.
*   **Digitization:**  Limited digitization means a lot of archival research is required.
*   **Data Format:**  Archival data is often unstructured (e.g., handwritten documents, letters).  Requires significant effort to convert into a usable format.
*   **Privacy:**  Data protection laws may restrict access to certain personal information.
*   **Completeness:**  Archival records are rarely complete.  Gaps and biases are common.




**To help me narrow down the search, could you tell me:**

*   **What specific aspect of German government/politics are you most interested in?** (e.g., political parties, specific policies, student movements, foreign policy, East-West relations, the legacy of Nazism)
*   **What level of detail do you need?** (e.g., national-level data, regional data, individual-level data)
*   **What kind of analysis are you planning to do?** (e.g., quantitative analysis, qualitative analysis, network analysis)



Good luck with your research!  It will be challenging, but potentially very rewarding.

Below is a selection of open‐data and research‐data sources that cover government structures, election results, party systems, parliamentary memberships and cabinet compositions in Germany during the 1960s. Most of these datasets refer to the Federal Republic of Germany (West Germany); you will also find pointers to comparable data for the GDR (East Germany).

1. DIP (Dokumentations‐ und Informationssystem für Parlamentarische Vorgänge)  
   • What it is: Official Bundestag system providing data on members of parliament, party affiliation, committee memberships and plenary protocols.  
   • Time coverage: Since 1949, including all legislative periods in the 1960s.  
   • Access/API: https://dip.bundestag.de/ (search for “Abgeordnete” or use the REST API documentation)

2. ParlGov  
   • What it is: Cross‐national database of elections, cabinets, parties and office‐holders.  
   • Germany coverage: Federal elections (1961, 1965), state elections, government coalitions and major parties (CDU/CSU, SPD, FDP).  
   • Download: https://www.parlgov.org/ (Data → Download)

3. Manifesto Project (CMP)  
   • What it is: Quantitative content analysis of party manifestos, with policy scores.  
   • Germany coverage: West German parties’ manifestos from 1965 onward.  
   • Access: https://manifesto-project.wzb.eu/ (Data → Download)

4. CLEA (Constituency‐Level Elections Archive)  
   • What it is: Detailed county‐ and district‐level results for national elections.  
   • Germany coverage: West German federal elections 1949–1990 (including 1961, 1965).  
   • Download: http://clea.voteview.com/ (choose “Germany”)

5. European Election Database (EED) – NSD  
   • What it is: Aggregated national and subnational results for European countries.  
   • Germany coverage: West German federal and Landtag (state) elections 1946–1990.  
   • Access: https://nsd.no/european_election_database/ (select “Germany”)

6. Destatis (Statistisches Bundesamt) Historical Time Series  
   • What it is: Official statistical yearbooks with data on government expenditure, civil service numbers, administrative structures.  
   • Time coverage: Back‐series covering 1950s–1970s.  
   • Access: https://www.destatis.de/EN/Home/_node.html → Publications → “Historical Statistics of Germany”

7. GESIS Data Archive  
   • What it is: Germany’s largest data archive for the social sciences. Contains surveys, election studies and other research datasets.  
   • Relevant series:  
     – “West German Longitudinal Election Study (WLES)” 1949–1990  
     – Various public‐opinion and elite surveys from the 1960s.  
   • Search/catalogue: https://www.gesis.org/en/home/

8. Wahlrecht.de (Election Law Archive)  
   • What it is: Expert‐maintained site with comprehensive federal, state and local election results, seat distributions, turnout, vote percentages.  
   • Germany coverage: All federal elections (including 1961, 1965) and GDR Volkskammer elections (1958, 1963, 1967).  
   • Access: https://www.wahlrecht.de/ (data is in HTML but can be scraped)

9. Bundesarchiv (Federal Archives) – Digital Collections  
   • What it is: Official repository for government records, cabinet minutes, organizational charts. Some collections are digitized.  
   • Coverage: Cabinet papers of Adenauer, Erhard and Kiesinger governments (1960–1969).  
   • Access: https://www.bundesarchiv.de/EN/Content/Navigation/Home/home.html (search the “Digital Collections”)

10. ICPSR / Harvard Dataverse  
   • What it is: International repositories that occasionally host German political data from the 1960s (e.g. East‐West German surveys, comparative cabinet data).  
   • Search: https://www.icpsr.umich.edu or https://dataverse.harvard.edu → search “Germany 1960 politics”

If you need more specialized data (e.g. internal party membership numbers, trade-union structures, local council compositions), you can also look in the RatSWD Data Catalogue (https://www.gesis.org/ratswd) or contact the archive of the relevant party (CDU/CSU, SPD, etc.) for their historical records.

prompt: Can you find datasets related to Government, political systems and organisations in Germany during the 1960s?
answer:
Yes — there are a number of open and commercial data resources that cover German government, parties, parliaments and political organizations in the 1960s (for both the Federal Republic of Germany/FRG and the German Democratic Republic/GDR). Below I list the best places to start, what each contains, coverage for the 1960s, formats/access and brief usage notes. If you want, I can fetch or download specific files for you.

Key data sources

- Bundeswahlleiter (Federal Returning Officer) — official election results
  - What: Detailed Bundestag and Land election results (votes by party, constituencies, seats), turnout, historical tables.
  - Coverage: All postwar federal elections (including 1961, 1965, etc.) and many state elections in the 1960s.
  - Format/access: Web tables and downloadable PDFs, some CSV/Excel extracts; site in German and English.
  - Link: bundeswahlleiter.de (search “Bundestagswahl 1961”, “Bundestagswahl 1965”)

- Wahlrecht.de — consolidated historical election data
  - What: Easy-to-use repository of federal/state election results, seat distributions, constituency-level breakdowns, by-election data and governmental coalitions.
  - Coverage: Comprehensive historical data for FRG and many GDR elections (including the plebiscitary GDR results).
  - Format/access: HTML tables, downloadable pages; frequently used by researchers for quick lookups.
  - Link: https://www.wahlrecht.de/

- Deutscher Bundestag — DIP / Open Data (plenary protocols, member lists, documents)
  - What: Plenary protocols (stenographic records), bills, petitions, lists of members of the Bundestag, committee information and government documents.
  - Coverage: Full parliamentary record for the FRG across the 1960s.
  - Format/access: PDF and (in many cases) machine-readable formats via the Bundestag’s Open Data services (DIP system for documents).
  - Link: https://dip.bundestag.de/ and https://www.bundestag.de/services/opendata

- Bundesarchiv (German Federal Archives)
  - What: Official government files, ministry records, cabinet papers, party files and other primary archival holdings.
  - Coverage: Extensive holdings for both FRG and GDR (for GDR there are parallel holdings and also the BStU for Stasi files).
  - Format/access: Archival catalogues online; digitized items are mixed; many files require archive request/visit.
  - Link: https://www.bundesarchiv.de/EN

- BStU (Records of the State Security Service of the GDR / “Stasi”) and GDR archives
  - What: Files relating to the GDR state security apparatus and related organization charts and lists (sensitive access rules apply).
  - Coverage: GDR political structures and organizations in the 1960s.
  - Format/access: Archival requests; some datasets and indices are published.
  - Link: https://www.bstu.de/EN

- GESIS Data Archive (Leibniz Institute for the Social Sciences)
  - What: Catalogue of social science datasets and survey data; also hosts historical election study material, party data, and metadata.
  - Coverage: Many German political datasets and surveys (search for 1960s topic keywords); host references and datasets used in political science literature.
  - Format/access: Downloadable after registration for many items; metadata searchable in English/German.
  - Link: https://dbk.gesis.org/dbksearch/

- ParlGov (cross-national database of parties, elections, cabinets)
  - What: Structured dataset of parties, elections and governments, including coding of government coalitions and parties’ attributes.
  - Coverage: FRG (West Germany) elections and cabinets for the 1960s are included.
  - Format/access: CSV/SQL downloads, API.
  - Link: https://parlgov.org/ (See “Data” section)

- Manifesto Project / MARPOR (party policy content)
  - What: Coded party program content (policy positions) for parties over time. Useful for studying party platforms and policy emphases.
  - Coverage: Major German parties in the 1960s (FRG parties are included).
  - Format/access: CSV, SPSS, STATA; public download after registration.
  - Link: https://manifesto-project.wzb.eu/

- Comparative Political Data Set (CPDS 1960–2018)
  - What: Country-year indicators (government composition, cabinet duration, parties in government, state structure variables).
  - Coverage: Country-level variables for West Germany (FRG) throughout the 1960s.
  - Format/access: CSV/XLS via the CPDS project page.
  - Link: https://www.cpds-data.org/ (or authors’ pages at University of Bern/Zurich)

- V-Dem (Varieties of Democracy)
  - What: Large cross-national dataset of regime and democratic indicators with yearly country-level measures and codings.
  - Coverage: Annual measures for FRG and GDR (coded separately) covering the 1960s.
  - Format/access: Downloadable CSV, STATA, R data files; extensive documentation.
  - Link: https://www.v-dem.net/en/data/

- Manifesto / party membership & organization statistics (secondary sources)
  - What: Historical party membership numbers and organizational statistics are often found in statistical yearbooks and scholarly compendia.
  - Sources: Statistisches Jahrbuch (Statistical Yearbook), GENESIS-Online (Destatis), academic histories of parties, and contemporary publications (e.g., party annual reports archived).
  - Format/access: Destatis GENESIS online (some data behind registration); yearbooks often PDF scans.
  - Link: https://www-genesis.destatis.de/genesis/online and Statistisches Jahrbuch archives

- GDR-specific published sources
  - What: GDR Statistical Yearbook (Statistisches Taschenbuch der DDR), official GDR publications of the Volkskammer, lists of mass organizations (FDGB, FDJ, DFD, VdgB etc.) and their official statistics.
  - Coverage: GDR political organisations and formal structures in the 1960s.

Useful research/search tips and follow-up help
- For election-level empirical work start with Bundeswahlleiter and Wahlrecht.de (for FRG) and with Bundesarchiv/GDR yearbooks or Wahlrecht for GDR elections.
- For party platforms/policy positions use the Manifesto Project; for government composition and cabinet data use ParlGov and CPDS.

prompt: Can you find datasets related to Government, political systems and organisations in Germany during the 1960s?
answer:
Here are concrete, citable datasets and data sources you can use for research on government, political systems, and organisations in (West/East) Germany in the 1960s. I’ll focus on datasets rather than just bibliographic references.

---

## 1. Election & Party Data

### 1.1 Constituency-level federal election results (Bundestag)

**Source:** GESIS – “Wahlergebnisse auf Wahlkreisebene” (Election results at constituency level)  
- Covers Bundestag elections including **1961, 1965, 1969**.  
- Variables: votes by party, turnout, invalid votes, constituency identifiers, sometimes socio-structural info.  
- Geographic scope: (West) Germany.

How to find:
- Go to: https://www.gesis.org  
- Search for:  
  - “Wahlergebnisse auf Wahlkreisebene Bundestagswahlen”  
  - Or in English: “Election Results at Constituency Level Bundestag”  
- Filter for data sets around 1960s; GESIS study numbers change, but titles remain similar.

### 1.2 GESIS Historical Election Data: Parties and Parliament

**Source:** GESIS “Politische Daten” series  
- Contains long-run **party strength, seat shares, coalition composition, government parties, opposition** etc.  
- Years: from founding of FRG/ GDR through the present, covering the 1960s.

How to find:
- Search on GESIS for:  
  - “Politische Daten Deutschland” or  
  - “Parteien und Wahlen GESIS”  

---

## 2. Public Opinion & Political Attitudes (Survey Data)

### 2.1 German General Social Survey / ALLBUS (for early 1970s onward)

ALLBUS itself starts in 1980, but:

### 2.2 GESIS Historical Surveys: West German political attitudes 1950s–1970s

GESIS archives a number of individual surveys run by institutes (e.g., Emnid, Infratest) during the 1960s:

Typical examples (names may vary slightly in English):

- **“Political Attitudes in the Federal Republic of Germany, 1962”**  
- **“Political Attitudes and Federal Elections, 1965”**  
- **“Political Culture and Democracy in the Federal Republic, 1969”**

Variables often include:
- Party identification, vote intention.
- Trust in government, parliament, parties.
- Views on democracy, Cold War, rearmament, Ostpolitik etc.
- Sociodemographics (age, gender, education, occupation, religious affiliation).

How to find:
- On GESIS, use the “Data Search” and search with combinations like:
  - “Bundesrepublik 196* Politische Einstellungen”
  - “Federal Republic 196* political attitudes”
- Then filter by:
  - Time: 1960–1969
  - Topic: Politics
- Many are available via download (registration may be required; sometimes affiliation).

### 2.3 Eurobarometer Early Waves (include West Germany from 1970)

For the very end of the 1960s / early 1970s:
- **Eurobarometer 1 (1970)** includes West Germany and questions on political institutions, European integration, etc.
- Not 1960s proper, but a useful benchmark.

Source:
- GESIS Eurobarometer: https://www.gesis.org/eurobarometer-data-service

---

## 3. Parliamentary & Legislative Data

### 3.1 German Bundestag Open Data (retrospective)

**Source:** Deutscher Bundestag – Open Data / DIP (Dokumentations- und Informationssystem für Parlamentarische Vorgänge)  
- Contains records of:
  - Bills, legislative initiatives
  - Parliamentary debates (Plenarprotokolle)
  - Committee memberships
- Much of the underlying data cover historical legislatures, including the **4th (1961–1965), 5th (1965–1969), and 6th (1969–1972) Bundestag**.

How to find:
- Start with:  
  - English entry: https://www.bundestag.de/services/opendata  
  - For the document system (DIP): https://dip.bundestag.de  
- Data formats vary; machine-readable data (JSON/XML) exist for many records. For the 1960s you may have to combine text data extraction and coding, but there are well-defined identifiers for:
  - parliamentary term,
  - party group,
  - committee.

### 3.2 Inter-Parliamentary Union (IPU) – Historical parliamentary data

**Source:** IPU Parline (older versions)  
- Not directly a micro dataset, but:
  - data on chamber size,
  - electoral system,
  - date of elections,
  - women in parliament, etc.
- Includes West Germany and GDR entries for the 1960s.

How to find:
- Current site: https://data.ipu.org  
- For strictly 1960s, you may need to check historical PDFs or older datasets referenced there; useful for institutional context rather than individual-level data.

---

## 4. Data on Political Institutions & Constitutional Structure

### 4.1 Comparative Constitutions Project (CCP)

**Scope:** Formal features of constitutions, including the **Basic Law (Grundgesetz)** and GDR constitution.

Variables:
- System of government (parliamentary, semi-presidential, etc.).
- Executive/legislative powers.
- Judicial review, rights provisions, etc.
- Can be filtered by “Germany (West)”, “Germany (East)”, time slices.

How to find:
- Website: http://comparativeconstitutionsproject.org  
- Look for the “Constitutional Data” or “CCP Data” downloads.  
- Time-series coding allows you to examine the institutional setup as of the 1960s.

### 4.2 V-Dem (Varieties of Democracy)

**Scope:** Very detailed institutional and practice-based indicators for democracy and governance, with yearly data.

For West Germany and East Germany, 1960s indicators include:
- Electoral democracy index, liberal democracy index.
- Party institutionalisation.
- Civil liberties.
- Judicial independence.
- Legislature constraints on executive.

How to find:
- Website: https://www.v-dem.net  
- Download: “Country-Year Dataset” and filter for:
  - Country: Germany (West), Germany (East)
  - Years: 1960–1969
- Data are numeric scores, not raw events, but excellent for studying political systems.

### 4.3 Polity5 or Polity IV

**Scope:** Macro-level regime characteristics.

- Polity score (−10 to +10), autocracy vs democracy.
- Executive recruitment, constraints, political competition.

Source:
- Center for Systemic Peace: https://www.systemicpeace.org/inscrdata.html  
- Download Polity5 or historical Polity IV; filter by Germany(West)/Germany(East), 1960–1969.

---

## 5. Party and Organisation Membership Data

### 5.1 Party Membership and Organisation Statistics (aggregated)

While micro-level membership lists aren’t public, there are aggregate statistics (often assembled in research-based datasets):

1. **Klingemann, Volkens, et al. – Party Organisation Data**  
   - Some datasets linked to the Manifesto Project and party research literature include membership figures and organisational characteristics for major German parties in the 1960s.

2. **Manifesto Project (MARPOR)**  
   - Not membership, but programmatic data:
     - Party manifestos for Bundestag elections (1961, 1965, 1969).
     - Coded into issue categories.
   - Very useful to connect parties’ programmatic positions to institutional context.

Source:
- Manifesto Project: https://manifesto-project.wzb.eu  
- Download the “Main Dataset” and filter:
  - Country: Germany (GER)
  - Election dates in the 1960s
- For each election/party you get coded issue proportions (governmental role, welfare, foreign policy, etc.).

---

## 6. East Germany (GDR): Government and Political System Data

### 6.1 GDR Elections and Institutions

Data types:
- Volkskammer election results by constituency/region.
- Official party shares (SED, bloc parties).
- Data on state structures, mass organisations (FDJ, FDGB, etc.)—often aggregated.

Where to look:
1. **GESIS** again
   - Search for: “DDR Wahlen Volkskammer 196*”
   - Some data exist on district-level results and political institutions.

2. **V-Dem, Polity, CCP**  
   - As above, with “Germany (East)” selected.

---

## 7. Cross-National Datasets Including Germany

These are not Germany-specific but provide **contextual political-system variables including 1960s Germany**:

### 7.1 Database of Political Institutions (DPI)

- Variables on:
  - Type of electoral system.
  - Effective number of parties.
  - Government composition, checks and balances.
- West Germany typically included from 1960s onward (GDR coverage is more limited).

Source:
- World Bank: search “Database of Political Institutions” or “DPI dataset”.

### 7.2 Cross-National Time-Series Data Archive (CNTS)

- Time-series data (1950+) on:
  - Government crises,
  - Executive changes,
  - Legislative elections, etc.
- Germany data for the 1960s.

This is a commercial dataset; access via institutional subscription.

---

## 8. Practical Search Strategy (if you’re working now)

If you tell me more about what you need (e.g., “panel data on parties”, “individual survey responses”, or “legislative events”), I can narrow to specific files with variable lists. But a general path would be:

1. **GESIS (for Germany-specific micro & meso data)**  
   - https://search.gesis.org  
   - Time filter: 1960–1969  
   - Topics: Politics / Elections / Government  
   - Keywords (German and English):  
     - “Bundestagswahl 196*”, “Volkskammerwahl 196*”  
     - “politische Einstellungen 196*”  
     - “Regierung Bundesrepublik Deutschland 196*”

2. **Institutional/Regime data (for comparative/system-level analysis)**  
   - Download V-Dem, Polity, Comparative Constitutions Project.

3. **Party-level programmatic and election data**  
   - Manifesto Project + GESIS election results.

---

If you tell me:

prompt: Can you find datasets related to Family life and marriage in Australia, Austria, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Cyprus, Czech Republic, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Great Britain, Hungary, Israel, Italy, Japan, Latvia, New Zealand, Northern Ireland, Norway, Philippines, Poland, Russian Federation, Slovenia, Spain, South Africa, Switzerland, United States of America during the 2000s?
answer:
Below is a non‐exhaustive list of both international and country‐specific data sources covering family formation, marriage and divorce, household and family structure in the 2000s (roughly 2000–2010).  In most cases you can download time series of crude marriage/divorce rates, counts of marriages, divorces, separations, numbers of families by type (e.g. with children, lone‐parent), and—where available—microdata.

1. Internationally comparable datasets  
 • UN Demographic Yearbook (UN Statistics Division) – detailed annual data on marriages, divorces, separations, average age at first marriage, etc.  
   https://unstats.un.org/UNSD/demographic/products/dyb/  
 • OECD Family Database – cross‐national indicators on family size, fertility, marriage/divorce rates, lone‐parent families, intergenerational co-residence  
   https://www.oecd.org/social/family/database.htm  
 • Eurostat Population and social conditions – marriages, divorces, households and family composition (for EU countries + Norway, Iceland, Switzerland)  
   https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/web/population-demography-migration-projections/data/database  
 • World Bank World Development Indicators – crude marriage rate, crude divorce rate (less detailed, but freely downloadable)  
   https://data.worldbank.org/indicator  

2. Microdata repositories (sample surveys & census extracts)  
 • IPUMS International – harmonized census samples from many countries (family and household relationship variables)  
   https://international.ipums.org/international/  
 • IPUMS USA – American Community Survey and decennial census microdata  
   https://usa.ipums.org/usa/  
 • Demographic and Health Surveys (DHS) – marriage and family modules, mostly for developing countries (e.g. Philippines, South Africa)  
   https://dhsprogram.com/  

3. Country-level statistical offices and major surveys  
Below are the national bodies and flagship publications covering 2000–2010 for each country you listed. Wherever possible you will find: annual vital‐statistics bulletins (marriage/divorce), family‐structure tables, and sometimes dedicated family surveys.

Australia  
 • Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS): Marriage and Divorces, Families and Households, Census family data 2001 & 2006  
   https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/people/people-and-communities  
 • Household, Income and Labour Dynamics in Australia (HILDA) Survey – longitudinal family, household composition, marriage transitions  
   https://melbourneinstitute.unimelb.edu.au/hilda  

Austria  
 • Statistik Austria: Demography – marriages, divorces, partnerships; Family Life tables  
   https://www.statistik.at/web_en/statistics/PeopleSociety/population/index.html  
 • Austrian Generations and Gender Survey (GGS)  

Brazil  
 • IBGE (Instituto Brasileiro de Geografia e Estatística): Demographic Census (2000, 2010), PNAD continuous – household and family characteristics  
   https://www.ibge.gov.br/en/statistics/social/population/  
 • (Limited DHS‐style surveys; most data through PNAD/census)  

Canada  
 • Statistics Canada: Vital Statistics – marriage / remarriage / divorce; Family Portrait; General Social Survey (family, marriage)  
   https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/en/subjects/Families  
 • Canadian Census microdata via Public Use Microdata Files (PUMFs)  

Chile  
 • INE Chile (Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas): Defunciones, Nacimientos y Matrimonios; Census of 2002, 2012 (household structure)  
   https://www.ine.cl/estadisticas/sociales/  

Cyprus  
 • Statistical Service of Cyprus: Vital Statistics (marriage, divorce, separation); Household and family composition tables  
   https://www.cystat.gov.cy/en/  

Czech Republic  
 • Czech Statistical Office: Vital Statistics; Family and Living Arrangements  
   https://www.czso.cz/csu/czso/home  

Denmark  
 • Statistics Denmark: Demography – marriages & divorces, cohabitation; families and households  
   https://www.dst.dk/en/Statistik/emner/befolkning-og-valg/befolkning  

Finland  
 • Statistics Finland: Marriages, divorces and partnerships; Family and household statistics  
   https://www.stat.fi/index_en.html  

France  
 • INSEE: Couples and families – marriage, PACS, divorces; Household surveys; Census  
   https://www.insee.fr/en/statistiques/2381474  

Germany  
 • Destatis (Federal Statistical Office): Vital statistics; Families and households; Microcensus (family structure)  
   https://www.destatis.de/EN/Themes/Society-Environment/Population/_node.html  

Great Britain (England & Wales), Northern Ireland  
 • Office for National Statistics (ONS): Marriages in England and Wales; Divorces; Families and households; Annual population survey  
   https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/birthsdeathsandmarriages  
 • Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA): Vital events, Families and Households  
   https://www.nisra.gov.uk/statistics/births-deaths-and-marriages  

Hungary  
 • Hungarian Central Statistical Office (KSH): Demographic Yearbook – marriages, divorces; Families and households  
   https://www.ksh.hu/?lang=en  

Israel  
 • Israel Central Bureau of Statistics: Vital statistics; Family and household characteristics  
   https://www.cbs.gov.il/en  

Italy  
 • Istat: Marriages and divorces; Family size and structure; Multipurpose Surveys on household  
   https://www.istat.it/en/population-and-households  

Japan  
 • Statistics Bureau of Japan: Vital statistics (marriage, divorce); Family Income and Expenditure Survey; Population Census  
   https://www.stat.go.jp/english/  

Latvia  
 • Central Statistical Bureau of Latvia: Demographic statistics – marriages, divorces; Household composition  
   https://www.csb.gov.lv/en  

New Zealand  
 • Stats NZ: Births, Deaths, Marriages and Divorces; Census of Population and Dwellings; Household Economic Survey (family composition)  
   https://www.stats.govt.nz/  

Norway  
 • Statistics Norway: Population – marriages, divorces, cohabitation; Families and households  
   https://www.ssb.no/en/befolkning  

Philippines  
 • Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA): Vital Statistics – annual marriages, divorces (noting divorce is very rare); Census of Population and Housing; Family Income and Expenditure Survey  
   https://psa.gov.ph/  

Poland  
 • Statistics Poland (GUS): Demographic Yearbook – marriages, divorces; Family structure in households  
   https://stat.gov.pl/en/  

Russian Federation  
 • Rosstat: Vital statistics; Family and household composition in censuses 2002, 2010  
   https://eng.gks.ru/  

Slovenia  
 • Statistical Office of the Republic of Slovenia (SURS): Demography – births, deaths, marriages, divorces; Household surveys  
   https://www.stat.si/StatWeb/en  

Spain  
 • INE Spain: Vital Statistics – marriages and divorces; Family characteristics from the Population Census 2001, 2011 and the Continuous Household Survey  
   https://www.ine.es/en/  

South Africa  
 • Statistics South Africa: Mid-year population estimates (including marriages), Census 2001, 2011; General Household Survey  
   https://www.statssa.gov.za/  

Switzerland  
 • Federal Statistical Office (FSO): Vital Statistics – marriages, divorces; Families and Households; Microcensus  
   https://www.bfs.admin.ch/bfs/en/home.html  

United States of America  
 • National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS): Vital Statistics of the United States – marriage and divorce rates  
   https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/products/vs.htm  
 • U.S. Census Bureau: Decennial Census and American Community Survey – household and family relationship tables; Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS)  
   https://www.census.gov/  

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
To summarize, you can:

1. Start with the UN Demographic Yearbook and OECD Family Database for comparable cross-national time series.  
2. For Europe (and Norway/Switzerland), Eurostat and the Generations & Gender Programme offer additional family‐demography data.  
3. Visit each national statistics office’s website for annual vital‐statistics bulletins (marriages, divorces), census family tables, and—if available—household surveys.  
4. Use IPUMS (International, USA) or national PUMS for microdata on family relationships and household composition.  
5. For countries where they exist, explore DHS (Philippines, South Africa) or other specialty family surveys.

All of these sources cover the 2000–2010 period, and many continue to update beyond 2010 should you need more recent data.

Cross‑national datasets covering the 2000s

  - Rich life-course microdata on unions, marriage, fertility, family networks.

Some others are close to 2009–2011; country participation varies and not every listed country fielded GGS in the 2000s.
- IPUMS International (2000/2001/2002 census round; microdata)
  - Household relationship structure, marital status, children in household, etc.
  - From your list, 2000s-round census microdata are available for many, including Brazil (2000), Canada (2001, 2006), Chile (2002), Czech Republic (2001), France (1999/2006), Hungary (2001), Italy (2001), Japan (2000), Latvia (2000), New Zealand (2001/2006), Philippines (2000), Poland (2002), Slovenia (2002), Spain (2001), South Africa (2001), Switzerland (2000), USA (2000; plus ACS 2005–2009 via IPUMS USA). Coverage for some others (e.g., Austria, Denmark, Finland, Germany, Norway, UK parts) may be limited or available via national portals instead of IPUMS.
- EU‑SILC (Statistics on Income and Living Conditions, 2004–2009 microdata)
  - Annual harmonized EU household microdata with marital/partnership status, household composition, children, basic childcare/time indicators.
  - Covers: Austria, Cyprus, Czech Republic, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hungary, Italy, Latvia, Poland, Slovenia, Spain (and the UK in these years; Northern Ireland included via UK files).
- European Social Survey (ESS), especially Round 2 (2004/05) Family, Work & Well‑Being module
  - Cross-national attitudes and self-reports on household division of labor, work–family balance, partnership context. Repeated cross-sections.
  - Many of your European countries plus (in some rounds) Israel. Good for attitudinal/behavioral correlates rather than full histories.
- ISSP “Family and Changing Gender Roles” (2002 module; also 1994 and 2012)

- World Values Survey (Waves 4: 1999–2004; Wave 5: 2005–2009)
  - Attitudes to marriage, family, gender roles; broad country coverage (includes many on your list: Australia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Czech Republic, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Great Britain, Hungary, Israel, Italy, Japan, Latvia, Norway, Philippines, Poland, Russia, Slovenia, Spain, South Africa, Switzerland, USA; participation varies by wave).
- Harmonized Histories (GGP side dataset)
  - Harmonized union and fertility histories derived from national surveys; subset of your countries (e.g., Austria, France, Italy, Norway, Russia, others) with life-course timelines suitable for event-history analysis.
- Aggregate indicators (annual)
  - OECD Family Database (marriage/divorce rates, family structures) for OECD members in the 2000s.
  - UN Demographic Yearbook and Eurostat Vital Statistics for annual marriage/divorce rates across most of your list.

Country‑specific mainstays in the 2000s
- Australia: HILDA Survey (from 2001; union status, marital history, children), ABS General Social Survey (2002, 2006), Census 2001/2006.
- Austria: GGS 2008–09; EU‑SILC; Microcensus Family modules; vital statistics.

- Finland: Population registers; EU‑SILC; Family Barometers; vital statistics.
- France: ERFI (GGS) 2005; EU‑SILC; Census 1999/2006; vital statistics.

- Great Britain/United Kingdom: BHPS (to 2008), Understanding Society (starts 2009/10), GHS/GLF, UK Census 2001 microdata (via UK Data Service), EU‑SILC; vital statistics.
- Hungary: GGS 2004–05; EU‑SILC; Census 2001; vital statistics.
- Israel: ISSP, Israeli Social Survey; Census 2008; ESS participation in some rounds; vital stats.
- Italy: “Famiglia e Soggetti Sociali” (ISTAT) 2003 and 2009; GGS 2003; EU‑SILC; vital stats.
- Japan: NFRJ 2003, 2008; JGSS (attitudes); JGGS mid‑2000s; Census 2000; vital stats.
- Latvia: GGS 2005; EU‑SILC; Census 2000; vital stats.
- New Zealand: Census 2001/2006 (unit-record samples); SoFIE (2002–2010); NZGSS 2008; vital stats.
- Northern Ireland: NI Life & Times Survey (attitudes); UK Census 2001 microdata (NI samples); EU‑SILC via UK; vital stats.
- Norway: Registers on marriage/divorce and family composition; EU‑SILC; ESS; vital stats.
- Philippines: DHS 2003 and 2008 (NDHS); Census 2000; Family Health Surveys; vital stats.
- Poland: EU‑SILC; Social Diagnosis panel (2000–2015) includes household/family measures; Census 2002; vital stats.
- Russian Federation: GGS 2004 (and 2007); RLMS‑HSE panel (household/union status); Census 2002; vital stats.
- Slovenia: GGS (early 2000s/late 2000s waves; check exact year); EU‑SILC; Census 2002; vital stats.
- Spain: EU‑SILC; Census 2001; national fertility/family surveys around mid‑2000s; vital stats.
- South Africa: DHS 2003; General Household Survey (annual); NIDS (from 2008); Census 2001; vital stats.
- Switzerland: Swiss Household Panel (from 1999); Census 2000; ESS; vital stats.
- United States: NSFG 2002 and 2006–2010; CPS Marriage/Divorce and Fertility supplements; SIPP marital histories; ACS 2005–2009; PSID.

What I can do next
- If you want, I can produce a tailored, downloadable list for just the countries you’ll actually analyze, filtered by:
  - Type: microdata with union/marriage histories vs. current status vs. attitudes vs. administrative aggregates.
  - Years: strictly 2000–2009 or allow 2010–2011 if needed for coverage.
  - Accessibility: open download vs. application required.

Here’s how you can get as close as possible to “time use in Duisburg during the 2000s,” and what actually exists:

## 1. Important limitation

Time-use surveys are almost never published for *one city* like Duisburg; they’re usually:

- national (Germany)
- sometimes by Länder (federal states, e.g., NRW)
- sometimes by region type (urban/rural, metropolitan areas)

So you probably won’t find an official microdataset explicitly labeled “Duisburg” unless:

- the dataset includes municipality identifiers (e.g., AGS / Gemeindeschlüssel) and
- those variables are not anonymized in the scientific-use file.

Most German official microdata suppress exact municipality for privacy, but you can sometimes approximate Duisburg via region variables (e.g., Ruhrgebiet / NUTS-3 / Raumordnungsregion).

---

## 2. Core German / European time-use datasets (2000s)

### 2.1 German Time Use Survey (Zeitverwendungserhebung – ZVE) 2001/2002

- **Producer:** Statistisches Bundesamt (Destatis)
- **Years:** 2001–2002 (in the 2000s)
- **Content:** Detailed 24‑hour diaries (activities in 10‑minute intervals), socio-demographics, household composition, etc.
- **Geography:** Germany; scientific-use files typically give region at a higher aggregation (e.g., East/West, maybe state groups or regional codes); municipality level is usually not included in openly accessible data.
- **Access:**
  - Documentation (German): search “Destatis Zeitverwendungserhebung 2001/2002 Wissenschaftsdatei”
  - Microdata via **FDZ der Statistischen Ämter des Bundes und der Länder** (Research Data Center):
    - Website: https://www.forschungsdatenzentrum.de/
    - Look for: “Zeitverwendungserhebung 2001/02 (Scientific Use File / Campus Use File)”
  - Access modes: guest stays, controlled remote execution, sometimes on-site at FDZ locations.

**Relevance to Duisburg:**
- You almost certainly cannot filter directly by “Duisburg” in the scientific-use file.
- You may be able to:
  - restrict to **Nordrhein-Westfalen** or to a Ruhrgebiet / metropolitan region code if provided.
  - use urbanization / settlement structure variables to approximate similar environments.

---

### 2.2 German Time Use Survey (ZVE) 2012/2013

- Just outside your decade window, but often used for comparisons.
- Same structure as above, accessible via FDZ.
- Useful if you want to show trends “around” the 2000s.

---

### 2.3 Harmonised European Time Use Surveys (HETUS) – Germany in the 2000s

- **Coordinator:** Eurostat / UNECE framework, national statistical offices implement.
- **Germany:** The 2001/2002 ZVE is part of HETUS.
- **Content:** Harmonised time-use diaries and background variables, comparable across countries.
- **Access:**
  - Eurostat’s microdata access (for researchers in eligible institutions):
    - https: // ec.europa.eu / eurostat / web / microdata / overview
    - Dataset: “Harmonised European Time Use Surveys (HETUS), around 2000 wave”
- **Geography:**
  - Usually very coarse: national or large region (NUTS1/2) – city-level not available.

**Relevance to Duisburg:**
- No direct city filter; at best regional approximations covering Nordrhein-Westfalen or larger regions.

---

## 3. Datasets that might have *some* Duisburg angle

### 3.1 German Socio-Economic Panel (SOEP) – time use modules

- **Producer:** DIW Berlin
- **Content:** Not a full 10-minute diary, but time-use *modules* in several waves (e.g., time spent on childcare, housework, commuting, leisure, etc.).

### 3.2 Microcensus / Mikrozensus (with working time, commuting, etc.)

- **Producer:** Destatis
- **Content:** Not time-use diaries, but:
  - working hours
  - commuting (time, mode)

- **Access:**
  - FDZ Mikrozensus:
    - https://www.forschungsdatenzentrum.de/en/microcensus
- **Relevance to Duisburg:**

  - Still not “full time-use” diaries but related.

### 3.3 Local / regional surveys in the Ruhr area

It’s worth checking for **city- or region-specific surveys** that might have time-use-like questions:

- **Institute for Work, Skills and Training (IAQ), University of Duisburg-Essen**

  - Search terms (German):
    - “Verkehrserhebung Duisburg 2000” / “Mobilitätsbefragung Duisburg 2005” / “Ruhrgebiet Haushaltsbefragung Verkehr”

Many of these datasets are not “standard” academic microdata; you may need to:

- the responsible research institute directly.

## 4. How to search systematically

If you’d like to continue the hunt yourself, here’s a search strategy that works well for German data:

1. **FDZ search portals**

    * **Demographic Data:**  Socio-economic background of VET participants.
* **Time Period:** The 2000s (2000-2009) is a key consideration.  Data availability tends to decrease the further back you go.



**I. High Probability / Easily Accessible (But may require cleaning & combination)**

Search for "Berufsausbildung" (vocational training), "Berufsschule" (vocational school), "Ausbildungszahlen" (training numbers), "Absolventen" (graduates), "Arbeitsmarkt" (labor market). These sources might have data, but it will likely require contacting them directly or digging through archives.

prompt: Can you find datasets related to Higher and further education in Uruguay during the 1960s?
answer:
Here are several places you can look for machine-readable (or easily tabulated) data on tertiary and post‐secondary (“further”) education in Uruguay in the 1960s. In many cases you’ll need to pull down PDF yearbooks and then extract the tables, or use a web-API to download CSV/Excel.

1. Instituto Nacional de Estadística de Uruguay (INE)  
   • Website: https://www.ine.gub.uy  
   • Look under “Publicaciones → Anuario Estadístico” for editions from 1960 to 1970.  
   • These yearbooks include detailed tables on:  
     – Número de alumnos matriculados por nivel (primaria, secundaria, terciaria, técnica)  
     – Número de docentes en educación superior y técnica  
     – Graduados por campo de estudio  
   • Most volumes are PDF; you can use Tabula (tabula.technology) or similar to extract the tables into CSV.

2. Ministerio de Educación y Cultura de Uruguay (MEC)  
   • Website: https://www.gub.uy/ministerio-educacion-cultura/  
   • Search their archival “Boletines Estadísticos” or “Balance General de la Educación.”  
   • Often these bulletins include post‐secondary vocational schools under “formación técnica.”

3. UNESCO Institute for Statistics (UIS) – Historical Education Data  
   • Data portal: http://data.uis.unesco.org  
   • Use the “Education” category, filter by Country = Uruguay, Years = 1960–1969, Level = “Tertiary” or “Post‐secondary non‐tertiary.”  
   • Key indicators you can download as CSV/Excel via the API:  
     – Gross enrollment ratio in tertiary education (indicator code SE.TER.ENRR)  
     – Total number of tertiary students (SE.TER.TOTL)  
     – Teaching staff in tertiary education (SE.TER.TCHR)  
   • Many of these series begin in the early 1960s.

4. CEPALstat (Economic Commission for Latin America and the Caribbean)  
   • CEPALstat portal: https://statistics.cepal.org  
   • Under “Social Indicators → Education,” select Uruguay and your years.  
   • CEPAL’s “Anuario Estadístico de América Latina y el Caribe” for the 1960s contains standardized tables for all member countries, often with slightly different breakdowns than INE.

5. World Bank – World Development Indicators (WDI)  
   • WDI portal / API: https://data.worldbank.org/indicator  
   • Relevant series (may start in late-1960s):  
     – Tertiary gross enrollment ratio (% of relevant age group)  
     – School enrollment, tertiary (% gross) (SE.TER.ENRR)  
   • You can also use the R package `WDI` or Python package `wbgapi` to pull Uruguay data for 1960–1969 into a data frame.

6. UNESCO Statistical Yearbooks (print/PDF editions, 1960s)  
   • UNESCO’s Digital Library: https://unesdoc.unesco.org  
   • Search for “Statistical Yearbook Latin America 1960” (and subsequent annual volumes).  
   • These contain regional tables with country columns—including Uruguay—on higher‐ed enrollment, graduates by discipline, etc.

7. University Repositories and Dataverses  
   • Harvard Dataverse, Figshare, Zenodo: search for “Uruguay education 1960” or “Latin America higher education historical.”  
   • Occasionally researchers upload cleaned time-series extracted from INE/UNESCO.

Tips for working with these sources:  
- Most 1960s material will be in scanned PDF. A combination of OCR + Tabula (or Camelot in Python) usually works.  
- Always check the metadata notes: classifications of “tertiary” vs. “technical” changed over time.  
- If you need fields of study (engineering, agriculture, humanities), UNESCO yearbooks and INE tables are your best bet.  

With these resources you should be able to compile year‐by‐year figures on:  
  • Number of students enrolled in tertiary and post‐secondary technical institutions  
  • Graduates by level and field  
  • Teaching staff and expenditure (where available)  
  • Gross and net enrollment ratios for ages corresponding to higher education.
