prompt: Can you find datasets related to Social behaviour and attitudes in Uruguay during the 1960s?
answer:
Below is a selection of the best available sources for social‐behaviour and attitude data in Uruguay around the 1960s.  As you will see, formal “public‐opinion” polling was still in its infancy in Uruguay at that time, so most of the readily accessible data come from censuses, household surveys and published statistical yearbooks rather than from continuous attitude‐tracking surveys.

1) IPUMS International – Uruguay Population Censuses  
   • 1963 and 1975 microdata samples of the Uruguayan population census  
   • Variables include household composition, occupation, education, fertility, migration, housing, etc.  
   • No direct questions on political attitudes, but you can proxy social behaviour (e.g. labour force participation, schooling, urban/rural residence)  
   • Access: https://international.ipums.org/international/  

2) Instituto Nacional de Estadística (INE) – Uruguay microdata repository  
   • Household Budget Surveys (Encuesta de Presupuesto de los Hogares) for 1963 and 1968  
   • National Household Income & Expenditure Surveys (ENCIG) series from late 1960s onward  
   • Labour Force module (men and women in economic activity)  
   • You need to register as a researcher; data are free but remote delivery may take a few days  
   • Access/general info: http://ine.gub.uy/ (look for “Microdatos – Encuestas de Hogares”)  

3) CEPAL (ECLAC) Statistical Yearbooks  
   • Anuario Estadístico de América Latina y el Caribe (1960, 1965, 1970 editions)  
   • Annual social indicators for Uruguay: literacy rates, school enrollment, health statistics, household size, fertility, mortality, migration  
   • Aggregated at country level—good for trend‐spotting in social behaviour  
   • PDF downloads: https://www.cepal.org/en/about/statistical-publications  

4) United Nations Demographic Yearbooks  
   • Vital statistics by country (births, deaths, marriages, divorces) back to the 1960s  
   • Useful for demographic behaviour and family‐related social indicators  
   • Access via UN Data Portal: https://unstats.un.org/unsd/demographic/products/dyb/dyb2.htm  

5) Roper Center for Public Opinion Research  
   • Although Uruguay polling in the 1960s was very limited, the Roper Center archives may hold isolated one‐off polls conducted by US government or academic teams  
   • Search by country and year; you’ll need a subscription or institutional access  
   • https://ropercenter.cornell.edu/  

6) ICPSR (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research)  
   • Search “Uruguay” + year range 1960–1970  
   • Possible leads: special‐topic surveys, comparative Latin American studies which include small Uruguay subsamples  
   • https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/pages/  

7) Newspaper archives and university libraries in Montevideo  
   • Major dailies like El Día, La Mañana or El País sometimes commissioned opinion‐polls in the late 1960s (often published only in the paper or internal bulletins)  
   • You can approach the Biblioteca Nacional or the hemeroteca at Universidad de la República for access to micro-films and any accompanying statistical bulletins  

8) Academic dissertations and published monographs  
   • Many political‐science and sociology theses of the 1960s and early 1970s included small‐scale surveys or interviews on topics like political culture, labour movements, student activism  
   • Check the Facultad de Ciencias Sociales (UDELAR) repository for digitized theses  

9) United States Information Agency (USIA) / Department of State archives  
   • The USIA occasionally conducted “Country Attitude Surveys” in Latin America during the Cold War  
   • These may include small‐scale questions on trust in institutions, perceptions of democracy, US relations  
   • Access via the National Archives’ online catalog or by FOIA request  

10) Secondary compilations / academic data banks  
   • Some specialized collections (e.g. the Latin American Data Base at Georgetown, or the Centre for Latin American Studies at Stanford) host scanned datasets or questionnaire facsimiles on discrete 1960s studies  
   • Contact the libraries or data services of major US or European universities with strong Latin America programs  

Important caveat:  
– Systematic, repeated public‐opinion polling in Uruguay (à la Latinobarómetro or World Values Survey) did not begin until the 1980s and later. If you are specifically after attitudes (e.g. political trust, ideology, values), you will likely have to work with one‐off surveys in archival form (newspapers, USIA, academic theses) and digitize them yourself.  
– For broader “social behaviour” (education, labour, household composition, fertility), census microdata and household‐budget surveys from INE and IPUMS are your richest, most readily available sources.

Systematic social science data collection was less common then, especially in Latin America. However, here's a breakdown of what *is* available, where to look, and what to expect, categorized by type and with links where possible.  I'll also highlight the challenges and suggest workarounds.

Don't expect much *direct* data from the '60s, but look for variables that might allow you to infer something.
*   **University of Notre Dame (Kellogg Institute) Archives:**  The Kellogg Institute has collected a lot of material on Latin America. They may have unpublished survey data or research reports from this period. It requires digging.
    *   [https://kellogg.nd.edu/resources/latin-america-data-and-archives/](https://kellogg.nd.edu/resources/latin-america-data-and-archives/)
*   **Archives of Political Parties/Organizations:** This is where you might find *some* quantitative data, though it's likely in a raw, uncleaned format.  You'd need to contact the archives directly. Examples:
    *   **Partido Colorado (Colorado Party) Archives:**  Might contain records of internal polling or membership data. (Contact information will need to be found through the party's current website/leadership.)
    *   **Partido Blanco (Blanco/National Party) Archives:**  Similar to above.
    *   **Partido Socialista (Socialist Party) Archives:** Again, potential for internal records.
    *   **Trade Union Archives (e.g., PIT-CNT):**  Records of union elections or membership surveys could provide insight into working-class political attitudes.
*   **Electoral Data:** While not attitude data, the official results of elections (presidential, legislative, municipal) are available. This can be used to *infer* shifts in political preferences.
    *   **Tribunal Electoral (Uruguay):** The official source for Uruguayan election results.  You may need to contact them to obtain historical data in a usable format.  [https://www.tribunalelectoral.gub.uy/](https://www.tribunalelectoral.gub.uy/)



**II. Indirect/Proxy Datasets & Sources (More Likely to Yield Results)**

These won't give you direct surveys of attitudes, but they can provide valuable context and allow you to reconstruct elements of political behavior.

*   **Newspaper Archives:**  Uruguayan newspapers (e.g., *El Diario*, *El País*, *La República*) from the 1960s are a *crucial* source. You can analyze articles for:
    *   Coverage of political events (demonstrations, strikes, rallies) – frequency and framing.
    *   Letters to the editor – expressions of public opinion.
    *   Reports of public meetings and speeches.
    *   Political cartoons – visual representations of attitudes.
    *   *Challenges*: Requires a lot of manual coding and qualitative analysis.  Digital archives may be incomplete or require subscriptions.
*   **Legislative Records (Diario de Sesiones):** The official records of the Uruguayan Parliament.  Analyzing debates can reveal the arguments used by different political factions and the issues that were considered important.
    *   [https://www.parlamento.gub.uy/](https://www.parlamento.gub.uy/)
*   **Government Documents & Reports:**  Reports from government ministries (Interior, Education, Labor) might contain data related to social unrest, political activism, or public services.
    *   Archivo General de la Nación (National Archives of Uruguay): [https://www.gub.uy/archivos-general-nacion](https://www.gub.uy/archivos-general-nacion) – This is the key repository for Uruguayan government records. You'll need to search their catalog.
*   **International Organizations Reports:**
    *   **United Nations:** The UN published reports on Uruguay during this period, particularly related to economic development and social conditions.
    *   **Organization of American States (OAS):**  Similar to the UN, the OAS may have reports on political and social developments in Uruguay.
    *   **Inter-American Development Bank (IDB):** Economic reports could provide context for political attitudes.
*   **Scholarly Literature (Historical Research):**  Look for academic books and articles *about* Uruguayan politics in the 1960s.  Researchers may have collected and analyzed primary source data that isn't publicly available as a dataset. (See section IV below.)
*   **Church Records:**  The Catholic Church was influential in Uruguay. Records related to social welfare programs or statements by church leaders could offer insights into public values. (Access is likely restricted; you'd need to contact church archives.)



**III. Specific Themes to Focus On (for Data Searching)**

The 1960s in Uruguay were marked by significant political and social upheaval. Focus your search on these themes:

*   **Rise of the Tupamaros (Urban Guerrilla Movement):**  Anything related to public perceptions of the Tupamaros, government responses, and security measures.

*   **Land Reform:**  A major political issue. Investigate debates about land distribution and its impact on rural populations.
*   **Political Polarization:** The 1960s saw increasing polarization between the traditional parties (Colorado, Blanco) and the left.
*   **The "Estado de Sitio" (State of Siege):** The periods of emergency rule imposed by the government. How did the public react?

*   **Access Restrictions:**  Archives may have limited hours, require permits, or have restrictions on access to certain materials.
*   **Data Format:**  What data you *do* find is likely to be in handwritten documents, typed reports, or old newspaper clippings.  You'll need to digitize and transcribe it.

*   **Bias:** Be aware that all sources have biases.  Newspapers may reflect the views of their owners, government reports may be politically motivated, etc.

prompt: Can you find datasets related to Political behaviour and attitudes in Uruguay during the 1960s?
answer:
Below is a list of leads and portals you can explore to locate survey‐ or election-style data on Uruguayan political attitudes and behaviour in the 1960s.  In most cases you will need to contact the archive or provider directly for microdata access, and sometimes you may have to digitize “paper” reports yourself.

1. Uruguay’s Electoral Court (Corte Electoral)  
   • Website publishes detailed official results (turnout, vote shares by party, by departamento and circuito) for the 1962, 1966 and 1971 elections.  
   • These are aggregate (macro) data, not individual attitudes, but can be invaluable for studying voting patterns by region.  
   • URL: https://www.corteelectoral.gub.uy/

2. Roper Center for Public Opinion Research (University of Connecticut)  
   • iPOLL database contains a variety of cross-national polls (e.g. Gallup, Eurobarometer-style) that sometimes include Uruguay.  
   • You can filter for country = Uruguay and year = 1960–1969 to see if any global or regional modules asked Uruguayans about trust in institutions, satisfaction with democracy, foreign policy attitudes, etc.  
   • Access generally requires an institutional subscription; free for some public domain items.  
   • URL: https://ropercenter.cornell.edu/ (look for “iPOLL”)

3. ICPSR (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research)  
   • Search their data catalog for “Uruguay” plus date filters.  Occasionally they host small Latin American surveys from the 1960s—often collected by North American scholars or international organizations.  
   • If you do find relevant holdings, you may need to request special access or visit in person.  
   • URL: https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/

4. FLACSO Uruguay (Facultad Latinoamericana de Ciencias Sociales)  
   • FLACSO-Uruguay was founded in 1968 and ran periodic public-opinion modules in the late 1960s and early 1970s.  
   • Their library/archive in Montevideo may hold original questionnaires and frequency tabulations (often in Spanish).  
   • You can write to their biblioteca or archivo de FLACSO-Uruguay to inquire about “Encuestas de Opinión Pública 1968–1972.”  
   • Contact page: https://uruguay.flacso.edu.uy/

5. Universidad de la República (UdelaR), Instituto de Investigaciones Sociales  
   • The Institute carried out occasional household and opinion polls in the 1960s on topics like party identification, economic evaluations, and institutional trust.  
   • Check their historical repository (some theses or working-paper series included raw tabulations or small sample surveys).  
   • Web portal: https://www.universidad.edu.uy/ (search for “Instituto de Investigaciones Sociales”)

6. CEPAL / ECLAC Surveys  
   • The UN’s Economic Commission for Latin America and the Caribbean sometimes sponsored “Encuestas de Condiciones de Vida” or social-attitude modules in the mid-1960s.  
   • Rarely full off-the-shelf opinion polls, but they can include questions on perceptions of government performance, social spending, etc.  
   • Contact the ECLAC library in Santiago or their Mexico City office.

7. National Statistical Yearbooks (“Anuario Estadístico de la República”)  
   • While these are aggregate, they often summarize periodic social surveys commissioned by the government (e.g. levels of satisfaction with public services, which between 1965–1970 sometimes included a few political attitude items).  
   • Available in PDF at the National Library of Uruguay or at the Dirección Nacional de Estadística.

8. Harvard Dataverse & Dataverse Uruguay  
   • Search for “Uruguay 1960” or “Uruguay public opinion” in both the global Harvard Dataverse and any Uruguay-hosted Dataverse nodes.  
   • You may uncover small data deposits by individual scholars who have already digitized part of the 1960s material.

9. Newspaper Archives & Secondary Compilations  
   • Some Uruguayan dailies (El Día, La Mañana, El País) occasionally reported poll results in their culture or weekend sections.  
   • The National Library of Uruguay’s hemeroteca may hold clippings you could tabulate yourself.  
   • Likewise, look for books on “Uruguay: cultura política en los sesenta” which sometimes reproduce tables of public‐opinion figures.

10. If all else fails:  
   • Identify key scholars of Uruguayan politics in the 1960s (e.g. Aldo Solari, Hugo A. Rodríguez) and scan their bibliographies for references to “encuesta,” “sondeo,” or “investigación de opinión.”  
   • Email them or their home institutions—many have kept private archives of the raw data.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
Next steps:  
a. Compile a list of specific questions you want answered (vote choice, party ID, trust in institutions, economic perceptions, etc.).  
b. Draft targeted data‐requests to the Corte Electoral, FLACSO-Uruguay, UdelaR’s Instituto, and the Roper/ICPSR helpdesks.  
c. In parallel, browse digital repositories (Roper iPOLL, ICPSR, Harvard Dataverse) with tight filters for “Uruguay” + “1960–1969.”  

With persistence you should be able to piece together both macro‐level election returns and at least a handful of attitudinal surveys from Uruguay’s tumultuous 1960s.

prompt: Can you find datasets related to Political behaviour and attitudes in Uruguay during the 1960s?
answer:
Yes — there are several places that either already provide usable quantitative data on political behaviour and attitudes in Uruguay in the 1960s, or are good sources to build such datasets (e.g., by digitizing newspapers, manifestos, or archived surveys). Below I list the most promising sources, what they contain, and how to access them.

Key national / official sources
- Corte Electoral (Electoral Court of Uruguay) — official historical election returns (vote totals by party/department/precinct, turnout, lists). Very useful for studying voting behaviour in the 1960s (e.g., national elections 1962, 1966). Access via the Corte Electoral website or by contacting their historical statistics office. Website: https://www.corteelectoral.gub.uy (look for “Estadísticas electorales” / “Resultados históricos”).

- Instituto Nacional de Estadística (INE) — national census microdata and socio-demographic statistics. Uruguay’s 1963 census is available and can be used to link socio-economic structure to political outcomes. For microdata access, check INE and IPUMS (see below). INE: https://www.ine.gub.uy

International/harmonized datasets and comparative indicators
- IPUMS International — harmonized microdata for many countries’ censuses. IPUMS includes Uruguay’s 1963 census microdata (household and person-level variables useful for studying the social bases of political behaviour). You must register for a free account to download. https://international.ipums.org

- Varieties of Democracy (V-Dem) — country-year indicators of democracy, civil liberties and many political-institutional measures that cover Uruguay across the 1960s. Useful for contextual, comparative analysis rather than individual-level attitudes. https://www.v-dem.net

- Polity / Polity5 dataset — regime-type and authority indicators for Uruguay in the 1960s. Good for regime-level analysis. https://www.systemicpeace.org/inscrdata.html

Electoral and party data / manifestos
- Nohlen / “Elections in the Americas” / official electoral statistical publications — compiled historical electoral data (vote shares, turnout) for Latin American countries, often used as a convenient reference for mid-20th-century elections. Many university libraries hold Nohlen’s volumes.

- International IDEA / Electoral databases — may have historical turnout and election timing for Uruguay. https://www.idea.int

- Manifesto Project / Comparative Manifestos Project (CMP) — content-coded party manifestos that can be used to infer party positions and the political offer in election years (check whether Uruguay’s parties/years in the 1960s are covered — coverage varies). https://manifesto-project.wzb.eu

Survey archives and public opinion resources (search/reach out)
- Roper Center for Public Opinion Research / iPOLL — the Roper Center maintains a large archive of public opinion polls; Latin American coverage for the 1960s is limited but check their iPOLL search for any Uruguay-specific polls. https://ropercenter.cornell.edu

- Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research (ICPSR) / Harvard Dataverse — search these repositories for any archived Uruguay survey datasets from the 1960s. Many country- or researcher-specific surveys are deposited there. https://www.icpsr.umich.edu / https://dataverse.harvard.edu

Primary sources to digitize / code (attitudes via content analysis / indirect measures)
- Hemeroteca and newspaper archives — major newspapers and weeklies (e.g., El País, El Día, Marcha) are invaluable for studying public discourse, letters to the editor, editorials and opinion polls (if published). The Biblioteca Nacional de Uruguay has digital and physical newspaper collections (Hemeroteca Digital). These are often the best source for attitudes and elite/public debate in the 1960s. Biblioteca Nacional: https://www.bibna.gub.uy

- Parliamentary debates and legislative records — Uruguay’s parliamentary records can provide statements, roll calls and elite behaviour to analyze political attitudes of MPs/parties. Check the Cámara de Senadores / Cámara de Representantes archives.

- University of the Republic (Universidad de la República) archives and theses — doctoral and master’s theses from the 1960s–1970s may contain original surveys or interview data (contact the social sciences department/library).

Other useful resources and tips
- If you need microdata on voting (individual-level votes), note that ballot secrecy may limit availability of individual vote records; aggregate returns are the standard available data.

- If you cannot find public-opinion surveys from Uruguay in the 1960s, consider constructing attitude measures indirectly (content analysis of newspapers, manifestos, parliamentary speeches, or oral-history collections).

- Library catalogs and national archives: Archivo General de la Nación (Uruguay) may hold archived survey research, NGO or party records, and unpublished polling from the period.

How I can help next
- I can search specific repositories for you (ICPSR, Dataverse, Roper Center) and report back on any Uruguay 1960s surveys that are actually archived there.
- If you tell me which kinds of variables you need (e.g., vote choice, party ID, trust in institutions, socioeconomic controls), I can prioritize sources (e.g., IPUMS for socio-demographics + Corte Electoral for vote returns; newspapers for attitudes).
- I can also help draft email text to request historical data from Corte Electoral, INE, Universidad de la República, or the Biblioteca Nacional.

answer:
Here are the main places and concrete datasets you should look at for political behaviour and attitudes in Uruguay in the 1960s. Because this is a fairly specialized historical topic, many data are in archives rather than in modern online repositories, but there *are* some usable leads.

---

## 1. World Values/World Handbook era surveys

### a) ICPSR (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research)

ICPSR is the primary repository for historical survey data in political science. They host a lot of 1960s Latin American surveys.

Actions for you:
- Go to: https://www.icpsr.umich.edu
- In “Find & Analyze Data”, search with combinations like:
  - `Uruguay 1960* survey`
  - `Uruguay political attitudes`
  - `Latin America 1960 survey`
  - `Latin American Public Opinion Project` and filter by 1960s
- Filter by:
  - Geography: Uruguay (or Latin America / South America)
  - Time period: 1960–1969
  - Subject: Political behavior, political attitudes

What to look for:
- National election studies or “political culture” surveys from the 1960s.
- Multi-country Latin America surveys that include Uruguay as a case (common for US-funded projects at the time).

You’ll often find:
- Voting behaviour
- Party identification
- Left–right self-placement, attitudes to democracy
- Union and political participation

You may need institutional access (university library) for some datasets.

---

## 2. Latinobarómetro historical predecessors / early regional studies

Latinobarómetro itself starts in the 1990s, but several earlier regional projects did surveys in Uruguay in the 1960s, often funded by US or European foundations.

Where to look:
- **Harvard Dataverse**: https://dataverse.harvard.edu
- **GESIS** (German data archive): https://www.gesis.org
- Search terms like:
  - `Uruguay survey 1960`
  - `Uruguay political culture`
  - `Latin America social survey 1965`
  - `survey Uruguay Montevideo 1960s`

You may find:
- City-based or national samples on political modernization, democracy, or civic culture.

---

## 3. Historical election returns (behaviour via votes)

If you can accept *aggregate* behaviour (votes, parties) rather than individual attitudes, there are better-documented data:

### a) Constituency-Level Elections Archive (CLEA)
- Site: https://www.electiondataarchive.org/
- Datasets: legislative and presidential election results at the district level.
- Filter for:
  - Country: Uruguay
  - Elections around: 1958, 1962, 1966, 1971
These give you turnout, party vote shares, etc., which are classic indicators of political behaviour.

### b) NELDA (National Elections across Democracy and Autocracy)
- Site: https://nelda.co/
- Focus: information about national elections (e.g., competitiveness, irregularities) including Latin American countries in the 1960s.
- Use to contextualize Uruguay’s elections and regime conditions.

---

## 4. Historical public opinion and elite surveys

### a) US and European-funded projects in the 1960s

Look specifically for:
- **“Civic Culture”–type or “modernization” surveys** in Latin America.
- These were often archived at ICPSR, Roper Center, or in specific university repositories.

#### Roper Center for Public Opinion Research
- Site: https://ropercenter.cornell.edu
- Search for:
  - `Uruguay` with date filter for the 1960s.
- Possible content:
  - US-sponsored or international comparative surveys asking about democracy, communism, US–Latin America relations, etc.
- Again, likely requires institutional access.

---

## 5. National and regional archives & statistical offices

For Uruguay specifically, some relevant places (even if not all are downloadable as ready-made datasets):

### a) Instituto Nacional de Estadística (INE Uruguay)
- Site: https://www.ine.gub.uy
- They hold census and survey microdata.
- For the 1960s:
  - Population census (e.g., 1963)
  - Labour force or socioeconomic surveys that may contain political or union-related questions.
- Look for:
  - “Microdatos históricos” or “censo de población 1963” and check questionnaires for any political variables (union membership, occupation linked to politics, education, etc.).

### b) Universidad de la República / Facultad de Ciencias Sociales (Uruguay)
- Political science, sociology, and history departments often maintain:
  - Digitized survey archives
  - Local election data
- Search:
  - `archivo de datos Facultad de Ciencias Sociales Uruguay encuesta 1960`
  - or contact: “Departamento de Ciencia Política” / “Departamento de Sociología”
- They may know of:
  - Early opinion surveys about parties, unions, student movements, or constitutional reforms (e.g., 1966 reform, 1967 constitution).

---

## 6. Specialized data collections on repression, conflict, and human rights

These are more 1970s (dictatorship era), but sometimes include late 1960s measures:

- **Varieties of Democracy (V-Dem)**: country-year indicators for Uruguay on:
  - civil liberties, party competition, participation
  - time series includes the 1960s
  - Site: https://www.v-dem.net
- **CIRI / CIRIGHTS / other human rights datasets**:
  - Country-year data on rights, which help infer general political climate and constraints on behaviour.

These don’t give individual attitudes but are good contextual indicators of political environment and regime characteristics.

---

## 7. What to do if you don’t find direct 1960s attitude surveys

Because 1960s Uruguay is a niche slice, you may find:

- Plenty of election results.
- Country-year indicators of democracy and rights.
- But fewer easily accessible individual-level attitude datasets strictly from that decade.

To still study “political behaviour and attitudes” you can:

1. Use **election data (CLEA, national archives)** for behaviour.
2. Use **V-Dem and similar** for macro-level attitudes/constraints.
3. Look for **early 1970s surveys** (pre-coup) as proxies for late 1960s attitudes.
4. Use **printed survey tables** in historical monographs, and reconstruct variables manually if needed.

---

- Help you craft very specific search strings for ICPSR / Roper / Dataverse.
- Help you design a proxy strategy (e.g., combining elections + V-Dem + early 1970s surveys).
- Or help draft an email (in English or Spanish) to Uruguayan archives or university departments asking for 1960s survey data.
